en.extract:
The Alor myzomela (Myzomela prawiradilagae) is a species of bird in the family Meliphagidae, the honeyeaters. It is endemic to the Indonesian island of Alor, where it is the only representative member of the genus Myzomela. It is named after Dewi Malia Prawiradilaga, an ornithologist at the Indonesian Institute of Science and one of the first leading female Indonesian ornithologists.


Taxonomy
It closely resembles and is most closely related to the Wetar myzomela (M. kuehni) of the adjacent island of Wetar. However, it noticeably differs in its calls and morphology.


Distribution
The Alor myzomela is restricted to a few montane areas of Alor Island, where it lives in Eucalyptus woodlands primarily composed of white gum (Eucalyptus alba) and Timor mountain gum (Eucalyptus urophylla), as well as wattles (Acacia sp.) and sheoak (Casuarina junghuhniana). This restricted distribution is in contrast to its close relative M. kuehni, which is among the most common birds on its islands; this difference may be due to the different habitat requirements of the two species.


Diet
They have been observed feeding on the fruit of Himalayan chokeberry (Photinia integrifolia) in small groups along with ashy-bellied white-eyes (Zosterops citrinellus). They also feed on the flowers of Eucalyptus alba, as well as occasionally insects.


Threats
This species is at high risk due to its severely restricted and fragmented distribution, which is compounded by the growing human population on the island. Thus, it has been proposed that the species be classified as Endangered on the IUCN Red List.


References

The '''Alor myzomela''' ('''''Myzomela prawiradilagae''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the [[Family (biology)|family]] [[Honeyeater|Meliphagidae]], the [[honeyeater]]s. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the [[Indonesia]]n island of [[Alor Island|Alor]], where it is the only representative member of the genus ''[[Myzomela]]''. It is named after [[Dewi Malia Prawiradilaga]], an ornithologist at the Indonesian Institute of Science and one of the first leading female Indonesian ornithologists.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Irham|first1=Mohammad|last2=Ashari|first2=Hidayat|last3=Suparno|last4=Trainor|first4=Colin R.|last5=Verbelen|first5=Philippe|last6=Wu|first6=Meng Yue|last7=Rheindt|first7=Frank E.|date=2019-10-05|title=A new Myzomela honeyeater (Meliphagidae) from the highlands of Alor Island, Indonesia|journal=Journal of Ornithology|volume=161|pages=313–324|language=en|doi=10.1007/s10336-019-01722-2|s2cid=203658792|issn=2193-7206}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.sci-news.com/biology/alor-myzomela-07665.html|title=New Bird Species Discovered in Indonesia: Alor Myzomela {{!}} Biology {{!}} Sci-News.com|website=Breaking Science News {{!}} Sci-News.com|language=en-US|access-date=2019-10-08}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
It closely resembles and is most closely related to the [[Wetar myzomela]] (''M. kuehni'') of the adjacent island of [[Wetar]]. However, it noticeably differs in its calls and morphology.<ref name=":0" />

== Distribution ==
The Alor myzomela is restricted to a few [[Montane ecosystems|montane]] areas of Alor Island, where it lives in ''[[Eucalyptus]]'' woodlands primarily composed of [[Eucalyptus alba|white gum]] (''Eucalyptus alba'') and [[Eucalyptus urophylla|Timor mountain gum]] (''Eucalyptus urophylla''), as well as [[Acacia sensu lato|wattles]] (''Acacia'' sp.) and sheoak (''[[Casuarina junghuhniana]]''). This restricted distribution is in contrast to its close relative ''M. kuehni'', which is among the most common birds on its islands; this difference may be due to the different habitat requirements of the two species.<ref name=":0" />

== Diet ==
They have been observed feeding on the fruit of [[Photinia integrifolia|Himalayan chokeberry]] (''Photinia integrifolia'') in small groups along with [[ashy-bellied white-eye]]s (''Zosterops citrinellus''). They also feed on the flowers of ''[[Eucalyptus alba]]'', as well as occasionally insects.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />

== Threats ==
This species is at high risk due to its severely restricted and fragmented distribution, which is compounded by the growing human population on the island. Thus, it has been proposed that the species be classified as [[Endangered species|Endangered]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
